title: Wildcard Matching (?, *)
description: Pattern includes ? matches 1 char, * matches any sequence. Example: adceb, *a*b → YES

examples.explanation: Start: i=0, j=0, p[0]='*'. Branch 1 (skip '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',0,1). p[1]='a', s[0]='a' match, i=1,j=2. p[2]='*'. Branch 1.1 (skip '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',1,3). p[3]='b', s[1]='d' no match, return false. Branch 1.2 (use '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',2,2). p[2]='*'. Branch 1.2.1 (skip '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',2,3). p[3]='b', s[2]='c' no match, return false. Branch 1.2.2 (use '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',3,2). p[2]='*'. Branch 1.2.2.1 (skip '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',3,3). p[3]='b', s[3]='e' no match, return false. Branch 1.2.2.2 (use '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',4,2). p[2]='*'. Branch 1.2.2.2.1 (skip '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',4,3). p[3]='b', s[4]='b' match, i=5,j=4, both end, return true. So overall true.
Start: i=0, j=0, p[0]='a', s[0]='a' match, i=1,j=1. j=1==len(p)=1, i=1!=len(s)=2, return false.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursion O(2^(n+m))
approaches.summary: Recursively try all possible matches by exploring both matching and skipping options for '*' characters, leading to exponential time. Use it for understanding the problem but not for efficient solutions.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Wildcard Matching
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution that explores all possible matches between pattern and string.
ladders.explanation: The brute force recursion approach solves wildcard matching by systematically exploring all possible ways the pattern could match the string. The intuition is that at each position, we have decisions to make: if characters match directly or via '?', we advance both pointers; if we encounter '*', we have two options—either skip the '*' (treat it as matching zero characters) or use it to match one character from the string and keep it active for potentially more matches. This creates a decision tree where we explore both branches for '*', leading to exponential complexity. The base cases handle when we reach the end of either string or both. The key insight is that '*' introduces branching because it can match any sequence, including empty sequences, so we must try all possibilities. The algorithm uses two pointers i (string index) and j (pattern index). If j reaches end but i doesn't, return false unless remaining pattern is all '*'. If i reaches end, return true only if remaining pattern is all '*'. For normal characters, match and advance both if equal. For '?', always match and advance both. For '*', recursively try: (1) skip '*' (j+1, i unchanged), or (2) use '*' to match current character (j unchanged, i+1). Time is O(2^(n+m)) in worst case (many '*'), space is O(n+m) for recursion stack. This connects to the full problem as the complete implementation. Common mistakes include not handling the case where string ends but pattern has non-'*' characters left, and incorrectly terminating recursion. Real pattern: def match(s, p, i, j): if j==len(p): return i==len(s); if i==len(s): return all(p[k]=='*' for k in range(j,len(p))); if p[j]=='?': return match(s,p,i+1,j+1); if p[j]=='*': return match(s,p,i,j+1) or match(s,p,i+1,j); return s[i]==p[j] and match(s,p,i+1,j+1).

examples.trace: Start: i=0, j=0, p[0]='*'. Branch 1 (skip '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',0,1). p[1]='a', s[0]='a' match, i=1,j=2. p[2]='*'. Branch 1.1 (skip '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',1,3). p[3]='b', s[1]='d' no match, return false. Branch 1.2 (use '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',2,2). p[2]='*'. Branch 1.2.1 (skip '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',2,3). p[3]='b', s[2]='c' no match, return false. Branch 1.2.2 (use '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',3,2). p[2]='*'. Branch 1.2.2.1 (skip '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',3,3). p[3]='b', s[3]='e' no match, return false. Branch 1.2.2.2 (use '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',4,2). p[2]='*'. Branch 1.2.2.2.1 (skip '*'): match('adceb','*a*b',4,3). p[3]='b', s[4]='b' match, i=5,j=4, both end, return true. So overall true.
Start: i=0, j=0, p[0]='a', s[0]='a' match, i=1,j=1. j=1==len(p)=1, i=1!=len(s)=2, return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Decision Function for Pattern Matching
ladders.desc: The recursive function that decides match at current positions with branching for '*'.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach—a recursive function that takes current indices in string and pattern and returns whether they match from that point onward. The intuition is that matching proceeds character by character, but '*' creates a fork in the road where we must explore multiple paths. The function handles three cases: base cases when we reach ends, direct character matching, and the special '*' case. For '*', it recursively calls itself twice: once skipping the '*' (meaning it matches zero characters) and once using it to match the current string character (and keeping it active for more). This dual recursion captures the essence of '*' matching any sequence. The step-by-step algorithm: function match(s, p, i, j): if j == len(p): return i == len(s); if i == len(s): return all remaining chars in p are '*'; if p[j] == '?': return match(s, p, i+1, j+1); if p[j] == '*': return match(s, p, i, j+1) OR match(s, p, i+1, j); return s[i] == p[j] AND match(s, p, i+1, j+1). Time per call is O(1) but total calls exponential due to branching; space is recursion depth O(n+m). This connects to L0 as the main recursive engine. Common mistakes: forgetting to check if string ends but pattern has non-'*' left, and not using OR for '*' branches. Real pattern: def match(s,p,i,j): if j==len(p): return i==len(s); if i==len(s): return all(p[k]=='*' for k in range(j,len(p))); if p[j]=='?': return match(s,p,i+1,j+1); if p[j]=='*': return match(s,p,i,j+1) or match(s,p,i+1,j); return s[i]==p[j] and match(s,p,i+1,j+1).

examples.trace: Call match('ab','*',0,0): p[0]='*'. Branch skip: match('ab','*',0,1). j=1==len(p)=1, i=0!=len(s)=2, return false. Branch use: match('ab','*',1,0). p[0]='*'. Branch skip: match('ab','*',1,1). j=1==len(p)=1, i=1!=len(s)=2, return false. Branch use: match('ab','*',2,0). p[0]='*'. Branch skip: match('ab','*',2,1). j=1==len(p)=1, i=2==len(s)=2, return true. So overall true.
Call match('a','?',0,0): p[0]='?', return match('a','?',1,1). i=1==len(s)=1, j=1==len(p)=1, return true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling '*' with Branching Recursion
ladders.desc: The recursive branching strategy for '*' that matches zero or more characters.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on how to handle the '*' wildcard, which is the most complex part of the problem. The intuition is that '*' is greedy but non-deterministic—it can match any sequence, including empty, so we must try all possibilities. The technique is to use recursion to explore two options at each '*': (1) skip it (match zero characters), which means move pattern pointer forward but keep string pointer same; (2) use it to match current character (and potentially more), which means keep pattern pointer same (since '*' can match multiple) and move string pointer forward. This creates a binary decision tree. The key insight is that by recursively trying both, we cover all possible lengths of sequences '*' could match. The algorithm: when p[j] == '*', return match(s, p, i, j+1) OR match(s, p, i+1, j). The first call explores skipping, the second explores matching one character and leaving '*' active. Time per '*' leads to exponential branches O(2^k) where k is number of '*' matches; space is recursion depth. This connects to L1 as the critical part of the decision function. Common mistakes: trying to match '*' in a loop instead of recursion, or not considering the skip option. Real pattern: if p[j]=='*': return match(s,p,i,j+1) or match(s,p,i+1,j).

examples.trace: p[0]='*'. Option 1 (skip): match('ab','*',0,1). j=1==len(p), i=0!=2, false. Option 2 (use): match('ab','*',1,0). p[0]='*'. Option 2.1 (skip): match('ab','*',1,1). j=1==len(p), i=1!=2, false. Option 2.2 (use): match('ab','*',2,0). p[0]='*'. Option 2.2.1 (skip): match('ab','*',2,1). j=1==len(p), i=2==2, true. So true via skip after using twice.

p[0]='*'. Option 1 (skip): match('a','*b',0,1). p[1]='b', s[0]='a' no match, false. Option 2 (use): match('a','*b',1,0). p[0]='*'. Option 2.1 (skip): match('a','*b',1,1). p[1]='b', i=1==len(s), but pattern not all '*', false. Option 2.2 (use): match('a','*b',2,0). i=2>len(s), base case: i==len(s)=1? No, i=2 invalid, but in recursion, i=2 would trigger i==len(s)? Actually i=2>len(s), so in code, we check i==len(s) first; here i=2, len(s)=1, so not equal, but we should handle bounds: typically i can exceed? In recursion, i increases only when matching, so i<=len(s). For i=2, it means we've matched past end, so in base case if i==len(s): check pattern left; but i=2>1, so not triggered, but in practice, call with i=2 would have i>len(s), so we need to check i>=len(s). Let's correct: match('a','*b',1,0): i=1==len(s)=1, base case: check if pattern from j=0 all '*', p[0]='*', p[1]='b', not all '*', so false. So overall false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Base Case Handling for String and Pattern Ends
ladders.desc: Determining when recursion should stop based on reaching ends of string or pattern.
ladders.explanation: This building block deals with the termination conditions of the recursion, which are crucial for correctness. The intuition is that we need to define what happens when we exhaust either the string or the pattern (or both). There are three scenarios: (1) If pattern pointer j reaches the end (no more pattern characters), then we must have also reached the end of the string (i at end) for a match; otherwise, it's false because extra string characters cannot be matched. (2) If string pointer i reaches the end (no more string characters), then the remaining pattern must be all '*' characters to match, because '*' can match zero characters, but any other character ('?' or normal) would require a string character to match, which isn't available. (3) If both reach end simultaneously, it's a match. The step-by-step: if j == len(p): return i == len(s); if i == len(s): check if all p[k] for k from j to end are '*', return true if so, else false. Time O(m-j) for checking stars; space O(1). This connects to L2 as it provides the stopping criteria for recursion. Common mistakes: not checking the all-'*' condition when string ends, or returning false when both ends reached (should be true). Real pattern: if j==len(p): return i==len(s); if i==len(s): for k in range(j,len(p)): if p[k]!='*': return false; return true.

examples.trace: i=3==len(s)=3, j=2==len(p)=2, both ends, return true.
i=3==len(s)=3, j=1, pattern left: p[1]='*', which is '*', so all remaining are '*', return true.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Matching Single Characters with '?' and Direct Equality
ladders.desc: How to handle matching of individual characters when no '*' is involved.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers the simple case of matching one character from the string with one character from the pattern, which occurs when the pattern character is not '*'. There are two subcases: (1) If pattern character is '?', it matches any single character from the string (except possibly null, but in our context, string has a character at current index). So we always consider it a match and advance both pointers. (2) If pattern character is a normal letter (e.g., 'a', 'b'), it must exactly equal the current string character for a match; if equal, advance both pointers; if not equal, the match fails at this point. The intuition is that '?' acts as a wildcard for exactly one character, while normal letters require exact correspondence. This operation is fundamental because it reduces the problem size by one character each time, progressing toward base cases. The algorithm: if p[j] == '?': return match(s, p, i+1, j+1); else (normal char): return s[i] == p[j] AND match(s, p, i+1, j+1). Time O(1) per call; space O(1). This connects to L3 as it handles non-'*' matching before reaching ends. Common mistakes: not advancing pointers after match, or mishandling '?' with empty string (but i is within bounds). Real pattern: if p[j]=='?': return match(s,p,i+1,j+1); else: return s[i]==p[j] and match(s,p,i+1,j+1).

examples.trace: p[0]='c', s[0]='c' match, advance to i=1,j=1. p[1]='?', matches s[1]='a', advance to i=2,j=2. p[2]='t', s[2]='t' match, advance to i=3,j=3, both ends, true.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion with String Indices
ladders.desc: Basic recursive function that compares two strings character by character.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces recursion in the context of comparing two strings from given starting indices. The intuition is to break down the problem of checking if two strings match from positions i and j into smaller subproblems: check if current characters match, and if so, recursively check the rest of the strings. This is a simple form of recursion without wildcards, focusing on exact character equality. The algorithm: function exact_match(s1, s2, i, j): if i == len(s1) and j == len(s2): return true; if i == len(s1) or j == len(s2): return false; if s1[i] != s2[j]: return false; return exact_match(s1, s2, i+1, j+1). This teaches how recursion reduces problem size by advancing indices, and how base cases handle ends. Time O(n) where n is length; space O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L4 as it extends to include wildcards. Common mistakes: not checking both ends separately, or off-by-one errors in indices. Real pattern: def exact_match(s1,s2,i,j): if i==len(s1) and j==len(s2): return true; if i==len(s1) or j==len(s2): return false; if s1[i]!=s2[j]: return false; return exact_match(s1,s2,i+1,j+1).

examples.trace: i=0,j=0: s1[0]='h', s2[0]='h' match, recurse i=1,j=1. i=1,j=1: 'e' match, i=2,j=2. i=2,j=2: 'l' match, i=3,j=3. i=3,j=3: 'l' match, i=4,j=4. i=4,j=4: 'o' match, i=5,j=5, both ends, true.
i=0,j=0: 'a' match, i=1,j=1. i=1,j=1: 'b' match, i=2,j=2. i=2,j=2: s1[2]='c', s2[2]='d' no match, return false.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Dynamic Programming Bottom-Up O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Build a DP table where dp[i][j] indicates if the first i characters of the string match the first j characters of the pattern, handling '?' and '*' with state transitions. Suitable for general cases with clear overlapping subproblems.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Wildcard Matching with Bottom-Up DP
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of wildcard matching using bottom-up dynamic programming with O(n*m) time and space.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to break down the matching problem into overlapping subproblems: does the first i characters of the string match the first j characters of the pattern? We build a DP table where dp[i][j] answers this question. The key insight is that '*' can match zero or more characters, which creates multiple possibilities we need to track. We initialize dp[0][0] = True (empty string matches empty pattern). For the first row (empty string), dp[0][j] is True only if all pattern characters up to j are '*'. For other cells: if pattern[j-1] is '?' or equals string[i-1], we look at dp[i-1][j-1]; if pattern[j-1] is '*', we can either match zero characters (dp[i][j-1]) or match current character and continue using '*' (dp[i-1][j]). The final answer is dp[n][m]. This works because we systematically explore all matching possibilities while avoiding recomputation through memoization. Time complexity is O(n*m) where n is string length and m is pattern length, as we fill an (n+1)×(m+1) table. Space complexity is also O(n*m) for the table. Connection to L1: This level implements the complete DP table filling logic described in L1. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization of the first row/column, mishandling '*' transitions, and off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: Initialize dp table with False, set dp[0][0]=True, handle first row, then nested loops for i=1..n and j=1..m with conditional logic for '?', '*', and exact matches.

examples.trace: n=2, m=1. Initialize dp[3][2]. dp[0][0]=True. First row: dp[0][1]=False (p[0]='a' not '*'). Fill: i=1,j=1: 'a'='a' → dp[1][1]=dp[0][0]=True. i=2,j=1: 'a'='a' but dp[2][1]=dp[1][0]=False. Final dp[2][1]=False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Table Filling Logic
ladders.desc: Implement the core DP transition logic for filling the table given initialization.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the main nested loop that fills the DP table after initialization. The intuition is that each cell dp[i][j] depends on three cases: if pattern character p[j-1] is '?' or matches string character s[i-1], we inherit from diagonal (dp[i-1][j-1]); if it's '*', we can either skip the '*' (dp[i][j-1]) or use it to match current character (dp[i-1][j]); otherwise it's False. This captures all possibilities: exact match with '?' or same character, and '*' matching zero or more characters. The algorithm: for i from 1 to n: for j from 1 to m: if p[j-1]=='?' or p[j-1]==s[i-1]: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]; elif p[j-1]=='*': dp[i][j]=dp[i][j-1] or dp[i-1][j]; else: dp[i][j]=False. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n*m) as it visits each cell once. Space complexity is O(1) per cell but overall O(n*m) for the table. Connection to L0: This is the main computational part of the full solution. Connection to L2: This implements the state transitions defined in L2. Common mistakes include incorrect index handling (off-by-one), forgetting to handle '?' case, and incorrect boolean logic for '*'. Real code pattern: Nested loops with if-elif-else structure checking pattern character.

examples.input: s='ab', p='a*', with dp initialized: dp[0][0]=T, dp[0][1]=T, dp[0][2]=T, dp[1][0]=F, dp[2][0]=F
examples.trace: n=2,m=2. Given initialized dp. Fill: i=1,j=1: p[0]='a' matches s[0]='a' → dp[1][1]=dp[0][0]=True. i=1,j=2: p[1]='*' → dp[1][2]=dp[1][1]∨dp[0][2]=True∨True=True. i=2,j=1: p[0]='a'≠s[1]='b' → dp[2][1]=False. i=2,j=2: p[1]='*' → dp[2][2]=dp[2][1]∨dp[1][2]=False∨True=True.

examples.input: s='aa', p='a', with dp initialized: dp[0][0]=T, dp[0][1]=F, dp[1][0]=F, dp[2][0]=F
examples.trace: n=2,m=1. Fill: i=1,j=1: p[0]='a' matches s[0]='a' → dp[1][1]=dp[0][0]=True. i=2,j=1: p[0]='a' matches s[1]='a' but dp[2][1]=dp[1][0]=False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP State Transitions for Wildcards
ladders.desc: Define the recurrence relations for DP cells handling '?' and '*'.
ladders.explanation: This level defines the mathematical recurrence relations that govern the DP table. The intuition is that matching is a state transition problem: state dp[i][j] represents whether first i chars of string match first j chars of pattern. For transition: if current pattern char is '?' or matches string char, we reduce both string and pattern by one (dp[i-1][j-1]); if it's '*', we have two choices: match zero chars (keep pattern, reduce string: dp[i][j-1]) or match current char and keep '*' for more (reduce string, keep pattern: dp[i-1][j]). Formally: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] if p[j-1]=='?' or p[j-1]==s[i-1]; dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-1] or dp[i-1][j] if p[j-1]=='*'; else False. Base cases: dp[0][0]=True (empty matches empty); dp[i][0]=False for i>0 (non-empty string vs empty pattern); dp[0][j]=True if all p[0..j-1] are '*'. This recurrence works because it breaks the problem into smaller subproblems with optimal substructure. Time analysis: Each transition is O(1). Space: We need to store previous states. Connection to L1: This provides the rules that L1 implements in code. Connection to L3: This builds upon the DP table structure from L3. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases, misunderstanding '*' transitions, and not handling '?' properly. Real code pattern: These are the conditional statements inside nested loops.

examples.trace: p[0]=='a' matches s[0]=='a', so use transition: dp[1][1] = dp[0][0] = True.
p[0]=='*', so dp[2][1] = dp[2][0] or dp[1][1] = False or True = True.

ladders.title: Building Block — DP Table Initialization
ladders.desc: Initialize the DP table with base cases for wildcard matching.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on setting up the DP table before the main computation. The intuition is that we need base cases to start the recurrence: empty string and empty pattern match (dp[0][0]=True); non-empty string with empty pattern cannot match (dp[i][0]=False for i>0); empty string with pattern can only match if the pattern consists entirely of '*' characters (dp[0][j]=True if all p[0..j-1] are '*', else False). This initialization is crucial because the recurrence relations depend on these boundary values. The algorithm: create dp table of size (n+1)×(m+1) filled with False; set dp[0][0]=True; for j from 1 to m: if p[j-1]=='*': dp[0][j]=dp[0][j-1]; else: dp[0][j]=False. Leave dp[i][0]=False for i>0 as initialized. Time complexity is O(m) for the first row initialization. Space complexity is O(n*m) for the table. Connection to L2: This provides the base cases needed for the recurrence in L2. Connection to L4: This uses boolean operations from L4. Common mistakes include incorrect handling of first row (e.g., setting dp[0][j]=True for any pattern), forgetting to initialize dp[0][0], and size errors. Real code pattern: Allocate 2D list, set first cell, loop through pattern for first row.

examples.trace: Create dp[3][3] all False. Set dp[0][0]=True. j=1: p[0]='*' → dp[0][1]=dp[0][0]=True. j=2: p[1]='a' not '*' → dp[0][2]=False. dp[1][0] and dp[2][0] remain False.

examples.output: dp[0][0]=True, dp[0][1]=True, dp[0][2]=True, dp[0][3]=True, others False

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison with Wildcards
ladders.desc: Compare a single string character with a pattern character handling '?' and '*'.
ladders.explanation: This level involves the fundamental operation of comparing one character from the string with one character from the pattern, accounting for wildcards. The intuition is that '?' matches any single character, '*' has special meaning but at single-character level we consider it separately in DP transitions, and exact match requires equality. For basic comparison (ignoring '*' for now): if pattern char is '?', it matches any string char; if pattern char equals string char, it matches; otherwise no match. This is a building block for the DP transitions where '*' is handled at a higher level. The algorithm: function match(char s_char, char p_char): return True if p_char=='?' or p_char==s_char else False. Time complexity O(1), space O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used when initializing or filling DP cells for non-'*' cases. Connection to L5: This builds upon simple character comparison from L5. Common mistakes include treating '*' as matching a single character (it doesn't—it matches sequences), and case sensitivity issues. Real code pattern: Simple if statement or boolean expression.

examples.trace: p_char is '?', which matches any character, so return True.
p_char is 'a', which does not equal 'b', so return False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Simple String and Character Comparison
ladders.desc: Compare two characters or strings for equality, a prerequisite for pattern matching.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic concept: comparing individual characters or entire strings for exact equality. The intuition is that before handling wildcards, we must understand how to check if two characters are the same or if two strings match exactly. For characters: use equality operator (==). For strings: compare length first, then each character sequentially. This foundational skill is essential because wildcard matching extends exact matching with special rules. The algorithm for string equality: if len(s) != len(p): return False; for i in range(len(s)): if s[i] != p[i]: return False; return True. Time complexity O(n) for strings of length n, O(1) for characters. Space complexity O(1). Connection to L4: This is the base that L4 modifies with '?' wildcard. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loops, case sensitivity oversight, and not checking lengths first. Real code pattern: Loop with index comparison or direct == for characters.

examples.trace: Lengths both 5. Compare: s[0]='h' vs p[0]='h' match, s[1]='e' vs 'e' match, s[2]='l' vs 'l' match, s[3]='l' vs 'l' match, s[4]='o' vs 'o' match. All match, so True.
Lengths both 3. Compare: s[0]='c' vs p[0]='d' mismatch, so return False immediately.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Greedy Two Pointer O(n+m)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers for the string and pattern, backtracking to the last '*' position when a mismatch occurs, allowing linear time by avoiding redundant checks. Optimal for most practical inputs due to minimal space usage.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Wildcard Matching with Greedy Two Pointer
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of wildcard matching using greedy backtracking to last '*' position.
ladders.explanation:
The greedy two-pointer approach solves wildcard matching in O(n+m) time with O(1) space by intelligently handling '*' characters. The core intuition is that when we encounter a '*', we don't need to try all possible matches immediately—we can greedily match as little as possible and backtrack only when necessary. We maintain two pointers: s_ptr for the string and p_ptr for the pattern. When characters match or pattern has '?', we advance both pointers. When pattern has '*', we record the position of '*' and the current string position, then advance only the pattern pointer (matching empty sequence initially). When a mismatch occurs, if we have a recorded '*', we backtrack: advance the string pointer from the recorded position and reset pattern pointer to after the '*'. This works because '*' can match any sequence, so if our initial greedy match fails, we can extend the match incrementally. The algorithm terminates when either: 1) Both pointers reach end (match), 2) Pattern has unmatched non-'*' characters (no match), or 3) String ends but pattern has remaining '*' (match after skipping them).

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize s_ptr = 0, p_ptr = 0, star_idx = -1, match_idx = -1
2. While s_ptr < len(string):
   a. If p_ptr < len(pattern) and (pattern[p_ptr] == '?' or pattern[p_ptr] == string[s_ptr]): advance both pointers
   b. Else if p_ptr < len(pattern) and pattern[p_ptr] == '*': record star_idx = p_ptr, match_idx = s_ptr, advance p_ptr
   c. Else if star_idx != -1: backtrack: p_ptr = star_idx + 1, s_ptr = match_idx + 1, match_idx = s_ptr
   d. Else: return False
3. While p_ptr < len(pattern) and pattern[p_ptr] == '*': advance p_ptr
4. Return p_ptr == len(pattern)

Time complexity is O(n+m) because each character in string and pattern is processed at most twice (once forward, once during backtrack). Space is O(1). This connects to the full problem by providing the complete solution framework.

Common mistakes: Forgetting to skip trailing '*', not handling empty string with '*' pattern correctly, infinite loops from incorrect backtracking. Avoid by carefully tracking star positions and ensuring backtrack advances string pointer.

Real code pattern:
def isMatch(s, p):
    s_ptr, p_ptr = 0, 0
    star_idx, match_idx = -1, -1
    while s_ptr < len(s):
        if p_ptr < len(p) and (p[p_ptr] == '?' or p[p_ptr] == s[s_ptr]):
            s_ptr += 1
            p_ptr += 1
        elif p_ptr < len(p) and p[p_ptr] == '*':
            star_idx = p_ptr
            match_idx = s_ptr
            p_ptr += 1
        elif star_idx != -1:
            p_ptr = star_idx + 1
            match_idx += 1
            s_ptr = match_idx
        else:
            return False
    while p_ptr < len(p) and p[p_ptr] == '*':
        p_ptr += 1
    return p_ptr == len(p)

examples.trace:
Step 1: s_ptr=0, p_ptr=0, star_idx=-1, match_idx=-1
Step 2: p[0]='*' → star_idx=0, match_idx=0, p_ptr=1
Step 3: s[0]='a', p[1]='a' match → s_ptr=1, p_ptr=2
Step 4: s[1]='d', p[2]='*' → star_idx=2, match_idx=1, p_ptr=3
Step 5: s[1]='d', p[3]='b' mismatch, star_idx=2 → backtrack: p_ptr=3, match_idx=2, s_ptr=2
Step 6: s[2]='c', p[3]='b' mismatch, star_idx=2 → backtrack: p_ptr=3, match_idx=3, s_ptr=3
Step 7: s[3]='e', p[3]='b' mismatch, star_idx=2 → backtrack: p_ptr=3, match_idx=4, s_ptr=4
Step 8: s[4]='b', p[3]='b' match → s_ptr=5, p_ptr=4
Step 9: s_ptr=5 (end), p_ptr=4 (end) → return True

Step 1: s_ptr=0, p_ptr=0, star_idx=-1, match_idx=-1
Step 2: s[0]='a', p[0]='a' match → s_ptr=1, p_ptr=1
Step 3: s_ptr=1, p_ptr=1 (p end) but s has more characters → return False

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking to Last Star Position
ladders.desc: Implement the backtracking mechanism when mismatch occurs with recorded '*'.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine handles the critical backtracking operation in the greedy algorithm. When we encounter a mismatch between string and pattern characters, and we have previously recorded a '*' position, we need to backtrack to extend the match of that '*'. The intuition is that '*' can match any sequence, so if our initial greedy match (matching as little as possible) fails, we incrementally extend what the '*' matches by one character at a time. We maintain two key variables: star_idx (position of last '*' in pattern) and match_idx (position in string where we started matching after that '*'). When backtracking: 1) Reset pattern pointer to right after the '*', 2) Advance match_idx by 1 (extending the '*' match by one character), 3) Set string pointer to the new match_idx. This effectively says: "The '*' now matches one more character than before, let's try again."

Algorithm:
1. If star_idx == -1: no backtrack possible, return failure
2. p_ptr = star_idx + 1  (reset to character after '*')
3. match_idx += 1  (extend '*' match by one character)
4. s_ptr = match_idx  (continue from extended position)

Time complexity: O(1) per backtrack operation. Space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the mechanism to handle '*' flexibility without exponential complexity.

Common mistakes: Not incrementing match_idx before assigning to s_ptr (causing infinite loop), forgetting to check star_idx != -1 before backtracking. Avoid by visualizing the extension process.

Real code pattern:
def backtrack():
    if star_idx == -1:
        return False
    p_ptr = star_idx + 1
    match_idx += 1
    s_ptr = match_idx
    return True

examples.input: s='abc', p='a*c', star_idx=1, match_idx=1, s_ptr=1, p_ptr=2
examples.trace:
Initial: star_idx=1 (position of '*'), match_idx=1, s_ptr=1, p_ptr=2
Step 1: Check star_idx != -1 ✓
Step 2: p_ptr = 1 + 1 = 2
Step 3: match_idx = 1 + 1 = 2
Step 4: s_ptr = match_idx = 2
Result: Now trying match with '*' matching 'b' instead of empty

examples.input: s='xy', p='x*z', star_idx=1, match_idx=1, s_ptr=1, p_ptr=2
examples.trace:
Initial: star_idx=1, match_idx=1, s_ptr=1, p_ptr=2
Step 1: star_idx != -1 ✓
Step 2: p_ptr = 2
Step 3: match_idx = 2
Step 4: s_ptr = 2
Result: '*' now matches 'y', pattern continues with 'z' at position 2

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Matching with Star Recording
ladders.desc: Implement the main matching loop with star position tracking.

examples.trace:
Step 1: s_ptr=0, p_ptr=0 → 'a' match → s_ptr=1, p_ptr=1
Step 2: s_ptr=1, p_ptr=1 → '*' → star_idx=1, match_idx=1, p_ptr=2
Step 3: s_ptr=1, p_ptr=2 (p end) but s_ptr < len(s) → loop ends
Final: s_ptr=1, p_ptr=2

Step 1: s_ptr=0, p_ptr=0 → 'a' match → s_ptr=1, p_ptr=1
Step 2: s_ptr=1, p_ptr=1 → '*' → star_idx=1, match_idx=1, p_ptr=2
Step 3: s_ptr=1, p_ptr=2 → 'c' vs 'b' mismatch, star_idx=1 → backtrack: p_ptr=2, match_idx=2, s_ptr=2
Step 4: s_ptr=2 (end), p_ptr=2 → loop ends
Final: s_ptr=2, p_ptr=2 but pattern not fully processed

ladders.title: Building Block — Pattern Character Matching with '?'
ladders.desc: Implement matching of individual characters with '?' wildcard support.
ladders.explanation:
This building block handles the fundamental operation of comparing a string character with a pattern character, where '?' acts as a wildcard that matches any single character. The intuition is simple: a match occurs if either the characters are identical, or the pattern character is '?'. This is the basic unit of pattern matching that gets combined with '*' handling in higher levels. When implementing, we need to handle boundary conditions: what if pattern is exhausted but string continues? What if string is exhausted but pattern continues? For this sub-problem, we assume we're only matching at valid positions where both indices are within bounds.

Algorithm:
def char_match(s_char, p_char):
    return p_char == '?' or p_char == s_char

In context of full matching loop:
if p_ptr < len(p) and (p[p_ptr] == '?' or p[p_ptr] == s[s_ptr]):
    # Match found, advance both pointers

Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This connects to L2 by providing the direct matching logic used in the first case of the core loop.

Common mistakes: Forgetting to check p_ptr < len(p) before accessing p[p_ptr], not handling '?' correctly. Avoid by always checking bounds first.

Real code pattern:
def match_char(s, s_idx, p, p_idx):
    if p_idx >= len(p) or s_idx >= len(s):
        return False
    return p[p_idx] == '?' or p[p_idx] == s[s_idx]

examples.trace: p_char == s_char → True
p_char == '?' → True regardless of s_char

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Pointer Advancement in Strings
ladders.desc: Implement safe pointer movement through string and pattern indices.

examples.input: s='x', s_ptr=1, p='*', p_ptr=0, advance_both=False (advance p only)
examples.trace: s_ptr=1 not advanced (at end), p_ptr=0 < 1 ✓ → p_ptr=0+1=1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Indexing and Comparison
ladders.desc: Basic string operations: accessing characters by index and comparing them.
ladders.explanation:
This is the most fundamental concept: how to work with strings as sequences of characters. In programming, strings are typically indexed starting from 0, so the first character is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. We can access a character at position i using s[i]. We can compare two characters using == operator. We also need to know the length of a string using len(s). These operations are prerequisites for any string matching algorithm. The intuition is that strings are like arrays of characters, and we examine them one position at a time.

Key operations:
1. Get length: n = len(s)
2. Access character: char = s[i]  (0 ≤ i < n)
3. Compare characters: s[i] == p[j]
4. Check bounds: i < len(s)

Time: O(1) for each operation. Space: O(1). This connects to L4 by providing the basic operations needed for pointer advancement.

Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing, accessing index equal to length (should be < not ≤), confusing string with list. Avoid by remembering Python/Java/C++ use 0-based indexing.

Real code pattern:
def string_basics(s, i):
    if i < len(s):
        current_char = s[i]
        return current_char
    else:
        return None  # or raise error

examples.trace: len(s)=5, i=5 not < 5 → cannot access s[5]
